trust-and-accountability-sign-language-a:
Trust and Accountability in Sign Language AI Innovation
Melissa Smith, Ed.D. 
Workshop Proposal 
Overview 
As sign language AI systems increasingly rely on large-scale video, translation, annotation, and  written-language representations to train avatar and language models, trust between the Deaf  community and technology companies has become a critical foundation for innovation. This  workshop examines how trust is built and sustained across the full lifecycle of sign language AI  development, from early, least-accurate models to real-world comprehension by diverse Deaf  users. 
AI development requires more than data. It requires human expertise at multiple stages, including  translation, cultural and linguistic annotation, video production, model review, and iterative  feedback. Deaf professionals play essential roles across these stages, yet Deaf identity alone does  not equate to expertise in all domains. 
Trust, Leadership, and Human Infrastructure 
Trust within the Deaf community is built over decades through accountability, relationships, and  respect for language and culture. Technology companies, however, often operate on rapid  development timelines. This workshop explores how leadership, vetting, and organizational  infrastructure can bridge these differing expectations while supporting responsible innovation. 
Equity and Access 
More than 80% of Deaf and hard of hearing individuals in the United States experience early  language deprivation, affecting comprehension, education, and access. Without intentional design  and review, sign language AI risks serving only highly bilingual or academically elite users. This  workshop emphasizes that “getting it right” requires designing for comprehension, not just  accuracy. 
Proposed Workshop Format 
25-minute presentation 
15-minute Q&A and discussion 
10-minute final reflections and takeaways 
Learning Outcomes 
Participants will: 
• Identify the types of human expertise required across the sign language AI lifecycle. • Explain how trust is built between the Deaf community and technology companies. • Recognize risks of designing AI systems only for elite bilingual users and articulate strategies  for inclusive comprehension.
1 
Trust and Accountability in Sign Language AI Innovation
Melissa Smith, Ed.D.  
Sign language AI is advancing rapidly. Avatar-based translation systems, sign language  recognition, and generative models trained on sign language video are increasingly visible in  research and product development. These technologies promise expanded access, faster  translation, and new tools for education and communication. At the same time, they raise critical  questions about trust, expertise, and responsibility—particularly for the Deaf community, whose  long history of oppression and inadequate definitions of inclusion have left many emotionally raw  and distrusting.  
Trust between the Deaf community and technology companies is not a given. It must be built  deliberately, sustained over time, and demonstrated through practice. Getting sign language AI  “right” is not simply a technical challenge; it is a quality of life imperative. 
Demand for Deaf Experts & Compensation Limitations 
Much of the expertise in the Deaf community are at capacity. Language and translation experts  who are highly bilingual, technologically sophisticated, comfortable in front of a camera, and  have business acumen or university resources are not sitting around waiting for work. The  quantity of video required to create data sets large enough for AI innovators to develop signing  avatars for a signed language will require proficient signers and filming and video sharing  systems that allow for short turnaround times.  
AI Needs More Than Data 
Not only do AI systems depend on massive data sets, data alone is not sufficient. In sign language  AI, human expertise is required at every stage of development: translation, video creation,  annotation, model training, review, correction, and feedback. Each of these stages demands  different skills, and no single individual or group can fulfill all of them. 
One misconception is that ASL fluency alone qualifies a Deaf person to be an effective model for  sign language production in educational or AI video development. In reality, fluency is only one  dimension of expertise. Not every native Deaf signer is a translation expert. Not every fluent  signer produces language that is accessible to a broad audience. Not every Deaf professional is  comfortable on camera, skilled at annotation, or prepared to work within technical production  pipelines. 
Furthermore, highly bilingual Deaf individuals—those who are fluent in both ASL and English  and who have academic or professional training—may naturally use more complex or academic  language. That language may be appropriate for them to participate fully in a variety of personal  
and professional contexts, but it is not automatically accessible to all Deaf users. Treating this  variety of language use as interchangeable risks building systems that work well for a small,  privileged subset of users while excluding many others.
2 
Questions that must remain central to ethical sign language AI development include: Who is this technology for? Who is the intended audience? 
More importantly:  
Who will be excluded given a specific interpretation?  
In the United States, more than 80% of Deaf and hard of hearing individuals experience language  deprivation in early childhood. This has lasting effects on language development, cognition,  education, employment, and access to information that open doors to opportunity. Many Deaf  adults do not read English fluently, and many do not use or understand academic forms of ASL  with ease. 
If sign language AI systems are trained primarily based on the needs and preferences of highly  successful Deaf professionals, they may be technically impressive while remaining functionally  inaccessible to much of the Deaf community. Accuracy, in this context, is an inadequate measure  of success. A translation can be linguistically correct and still be unintelligible to its intended  audience. 
Getting it right means designing for comprehension, not just performance. 
Trust is Earned Over Time 
Within the Deaf community, trust is built slowly. It is grounded in long-standing relationships,  accountability, and demonstrated respect for sign language and culture. Many Deaf people have  experienced generations of exclusion, misrepresentation, and broken promises from institutions  
that claimed to serve them. Skepticism toward new technologies—especially those developed by  people outside the community—is not resistance to innovation; it is a rational response to history. Technology companies, by contrast, often operate on compressed timelines. Iteration is rapid.  Products are launched, revised, and sometimes abandoned within months. These differing  expectations can create friction, particularly when sign language AI projects require deep  community engagement and long-term commitment. 
Bridging this gap requires leadership, organizational infrastructure, and a willingness to prioritize  relationships alongside innovation. Trust does not live in mission statements. It lives in systems:  in who is hired, who reviews work, who has decision-making power, and who is accountable  when things go wrong. 
The Role of Organizations and Leadership 
Organizations that serve as intermediaries between the Deaf community and technology  companies play a critical role in building trust. These organizations must be able to vet expertise,  broker relationships, and support Deaf professionals across a range of roles. They must be  responsive in both sign language and written languages, capable of working across cultural and  national boundaries, and prepared to operate within corporate development environments without  abandoning community-centered values. 
Leadership matter, as does transparency. AI development is inherently iterative, and early models  are often inaccurate. A culture that treats mistakes as failures rather than expected steps in  learning risks hiding problems instead of correcting them. Getting it right requires openness,  feedback, and the humility to revise. This process is likely to terrify the Deaf community. Will 
3 
this be yet another technology that creates more barriers rather than opening doors?  
Garbage In, Unintelligible Out 
The principle is simple: AI systems reflect the data and assumptions they are built on. If training  data is narrow, unrepresentative, or poorly reviewed, the output will be similarly limited. In sign  language AI, this can mean avatars that look fluent but communicate poorly, translations that  prioritize English structure over sign language grammar, or models that fail to account for  regional, cultural, or educational variation. 
Human review is not an optional add-on. It is essential infrastructure. Deaf reviewers at multiple  stages—especially during early, least-accurate phases—are critical to ensuring that systems  improve rather than entrench errors. Review must include Deaf individuals with diverse language  backgrounds, not only those who are highly bilingual or academically trained. 
Getting it Right is Dynamic 
Getting sign language AI right is not a moment of technical success. It is an ongoing relationship  between communities, experts, and innovators. It requires recognizing that trust must be earned,  that expertise is specialized, and that access must be measured by understanding rather than  prestige. 
Sign language AI has the potential to expand access, reduce language deprivation, and support  communication in powerful ways. Realizing that potential depends on whether developers and  organizations are willing to slow down where necessary, listen carefully, and build systems that  serve the full diversity of the Deaf community. 
Innovation moves fast. Trust does not. Getting it right means honoring both.
lessons-dataset-creation-sustainable-sl-:
Format: Workshop 
Title: Lessons from Dataset Creation for Sustainable Sign Language AI
Description: This workshop shares lessons learned from working with interpreters on large-scale sign language dataset creation, and why those lessons matter for building sustainable sign language AI.
Drawing on real-world experience in accessibility and AI-adjacent work—including outcomes from speech-to-text deployment in captioning—the session examines how early dataset decisions encode assumptions about professional labor, variation, and appropriate use. These assumptions often shape model evaluation, product claims, and deployment decisions, with long-term consequences for access quality and workforce sustainability.
The workshop centers interpreter perspectives during dataset creation and the realities that shape the data AI systems learn from. It shows how integrating interpreter expertise into dataset and downstream decisions supports responsible and sustainable sign language AI.
Presenter(s): Brian Birnbaum, Daniel Sommer
Email: brian.birnbaum@bisworld.com, daniel.sommer@bisworld.com
Brief presenter bios
Brian Birnbaum is a CODA whose father, David, was the first Deaf founder and owner of a nationwide sign language interpreting agency. 30 years later, Brian now runs Birnbaum Interpreting Services. He has leveraged his extensive experience with Deaf access systems at scale to build a streamlined data aggregation platform. His work emphasizes ethical practice, professional sustainability, and the responsible integration of emerging technologies into sign language access.
Daniel Sommer works at the intersection of accessibility, language, and AI-adjacent systems. He began his career in real-time and post-production captioning and now works in project management and technology across captioning, audio description, and ASL localization, with accessibility guiding system design and decision-making.
Learning Objectives: 
Understand how dataset creation encodes assumptions that influence AI training, evaluation, and real-world deployment
Learn how interpreter lived experience during dataset creation provides critical context that data alone cannot capture
Identify risks of misuse and over-promotion of sign language AI tools based on lessons from accessibility history
Explore how keeping interpreter expertise in dialogue with AI teams supports responsible use and professional sustainability
Technical Requirements: Projector (Slideshow)
practical-applications-ai-sl-translation:
Title and Description: Practical Applications of AI Sign Language Translation
AI sign language translation is advancing rapidly - but what does the Deaf community actually need from it? This workshop showcases Signapse's latest photo-realistic BSL and ASL generation technology, then opens a collaborative discussion on how it should be applied. Participants will explore which use cases (live translation, pre-recorded content, public information, and beyond) best serve Deaf communities, and how centering Deaf perspectives in AI and product development leads to technology that truly meets real-world needs.
Presenter(s): Ben Saunders, ben@signapse.ai, Signapse & Marcus Oaten, marcus@signapse.ai, Signapse
Brief presenter bio: Dr Ben Saunders is Co-Founder and Chief Scientist at Signapse, a UK-based company developing photo-realistic sign language translation using artificial intelligence. He completed his PhD in Artificial Intelligence at the University of Surrey’s Centre for Vision, Speech and Signal Processing. His doctoral and postdoctoral research has focused on AI-generated sign language production, translating spoken or written input into continuous, human-like signed output in both British and American Sign Language.
Learning Objectives: 
Participants will:
See the state of the art in photo-realistic BSL and ASL generation and what's now technically possible.
Debate where this technology helps most e.g. live translation, pre-recorded content, or beyond, and where human interpreters remain essential.
Learn how to build AI with the Deaf community, not just for them, through co-design and ongoing involvement.
Technical Requirements: Use of a screen
bridging-gap-ai-avatars-sign-language-an:
Format: Tech Showcase / Presentation
Duration: 30–40 minutes
Title: Bridging the Gap: Real-Time AI Avatars & Sign Language Animation
Description: This session will introduce a novel approach to automated sign language generation, developed by vosia.ai. We will demonstrate the capabilities of our AI-powered engine which creates real-time, high-fidelity 3D sign language avatars. Instead of a deep-dive into proprietary algorithms, the discussion will focus on the high-level approach to "text-to-sign" conversion, performance benchmarks, and the unique challenges of processing authentic mimic/gesture nuances. As a native signer and computer engineer, Dr. Uyanık will also discuss the importance of community-informed design in building accessible AI tools.
Presenters:
Name: Egehan Karabulut
Email: egehann46@hotmail.com
Organization: Vosia.ai
Brief Bio: Egehan Karabulut is a senior student in Management Information Systems at Sakarya University and Co-Founder of Vosia.ai. He is involved in product strategy, technical development coordination, and the implementation of AI-powered systems, interactive interfaces, and 3D avatar technologies. His areas of expertise include Python, JavaScript, React, Three.js, natural language processing, computer vision, machine learning, SQL, and Blender.

Name: Dr. Burak Uyanık
Email: dr.buyanik@gmail.com
Organization: Vosia.ai
Role: Lead Researcher & Core Team Member
Brief Bio: Dr. Burak Uyanık is a deaf software engineer, researcher, and civil society leader working in accessibility, education, and technology. He completed his PhD in Computer Engineering in 2023 and is recognized as the first hearing-impaired computer engineer in Türkiye to complete a doctoral degree. He has worked in software, automation, AI, and R&D across industry and public-sector institutions, and he is also the President of the Association of Hearing Impaired Solidarity, where he leads projects focused on accessibility, STEM, and inclusive participation.
Language/Access Note: Dr. Uyanık is Deaf and will deliver his presentation in International Sign (IS). (We kindly request confirmation on the availability of voice interpreters for the non-signing audience).
Learning Objectives:
-Examine the key challenges and solutions in real-time 3D sign language generation (e.g., latency, fluidity).
-Gain insights from a Deaf technologist's perspective on AI-driven accessibility.
-Identify practical strategies for ensuring semantic accuracy in avatar-based communication.
human-ai-collaboration-sign-language-tec:
Human-AI Collaboration in Sign Language Tech: Toward Equitable Communication”
Description:Delve into how AI can complement — not substitute — human expertise in sign language communication. From real-time recognition engines to avatar systems and seamless customer support experiences, this session highlights approaches that balance automated tools, ethical data use, and human cultural expertise. Attendees will walk away with design principles and case studies that reflect inclusive AI development at the cutting edge.Best for AI ethics researchers, accessibility technologists, and developers.
Presenter(s): Craig Radford and Brandon Dopf
craig@360directaccess.com
brandon@360directaccess.com
Bio: 
Craig Radford is a nationally recognized business leader, motivational speaker, and cofounder and CEO of 360 Direct Access. With over 25 years of experience advancing communication equity, he has pioneered innovations in Video Relay Service (VRS), Video Remote Interpreting (VRI), and Direct Video Calling (DVC).
Under his leadership, 360 Direct Access has grown over 600 percent in three years, partnering with global brands like LG Electronics and enabling the first U.S. bank to offer direct video access in American Sign Language. The company is expanding internationally with services in six additional sign languages and accessibility launches planned alongside Fortune 100 companies.
Craig’s earlier work in hospital-based VRI helped fuel the rise of Stratus Video, acquired by AMN Healthcare in 2020 for $475 million. Today, he is shaping the future of AI in accessibility and healthcare through acquisitions, federal SBIR funding, and new AI-driven sign language recognition technologies.
A serial entrepreneur with three successful exits including one nearing $1 billion, Craig has been featured in Forbes, Business Insider, and CNN. He also serves as Executive Director of USA Deaf Basketball, preparing U.S. national teams for the 2025 Deaflympics. Previously, he was on the coaching staff for the USA Deaf Men’s Basketball Team, where he helped lead the team to a gold medal in one year and a silver medal in another. Known for his clear and practical insights, Craig inspires audiences worldwide to build inclusive and future ready organizations.
Learning objectives:
Describe how AI sign language recognition and avatar technologies are used in real-world accessibility solutions.
Apply Deaf-centered and human-centered design principles to AI system development.
Recognize ethical and cultural risks in sign language AI and identify strategies to mitigate them.
Identify opportunities for responsible human-AI collaboration in accessible communication.
Tech requirements - Projector and screen
learning-with-signers-educational-slxai:
Format: Panel
Duration: 50 minutes
Title and Description:
Learning with Signers: Educational Applications of SLxAI
Today, many students use AI to assist their learning process, for example by finding scholarly articles and answering questions about those articles. What would it look like to make this application of AI more accessible to students who prefer a sign language? This is a complex goal which requires collaboration between teachers, students, researchers, and the AI systems themselves. In this panel, researchers in educational neuroscience and SLxAI will share their perspectives on how to facilitate these collaborations and advance the state-of-the-art for SLxAI learning tools. Panelists will highlight core issues and ongoing work at this intersection, including (a) augmented reality interface design, (b) training sign language models to include domain-specific terms, and (c) evaluating student outcomes. Audience will be encouraged to give their questions and comments throughout the panel.
Presenter(s):
Name, email, organization
Lee Kezar, lee.kezar@gallaudet.edu, Gallaudet University
Lorna Quandt, lorna.quandt@gallaudet.edu, Gallaudet University
Athena Willis, athena.s.willis@gmail.com, independent researcher
Laurel Aichler, laurel.aichler@gallaudet.edu, Gallaudet University
Brief presenter bio
Dr. Lee Kezar is a computer scientist and postdoctoral researcher at Gallaudet University with a focus on linguistically-informed machine learning models of American Sign Language.
Dr. Athena Willis is an independent researcher in HCI and educational neuroscience. She currently researches the design and evaluation of AI and augmented reality systems that collaborate with signing users for intellectual and language development.
Dr. Lorna Quandt is an associate professor of Educational Neuroscience at Gallaudet University. Her work examines the intersection of learning science and neuroscience by examining how emerging technologies can facilitate education for sign language users. 
Laurel Aichler is a first year student in the PhD in Educational Neuroscience program at Gallaudet and a research assistant in the Action and Brain lab under Dr. Quandt. She has an M.A. in Linguistics (emph. sign languages) and was an ASL interpreter for about a decade. She is part of a project focusing on STEM learning for signing users.
LLearning Objectives: 
Develop a nuanced understanding of how educational technologies can meaningfully include DHH students.
Gain varying perspectives on the challenges associated with educational technology for sign languages.
Become familiar with ongoing research in educational technology in DHH classrooms.
Technical Requirements: Projector for slides
good-enough-for-whom-ethics-power-accoun:
Good enough for whom? Ethics, power, and accountability in sign language AI deployment
Description
Sign language AI risks to be deployed before it is linguistically, socially, and culturally ready, and once a system is seen as “good enough”, institutions may treat it as default access – quietly reshaping choices, standards, and rights in practice. This workshop argues that technical and commercial decisions become standards: dataset provenance and inclusion rules determine what signing becomes learnable by AI; benchmarks define “quality”; and procurement contracts define what counts as “supported language” and “sufficient access”.
To make this practical, we’ll do two hands-on activities. First, a deaf user journey map: participants map a real communication scenario (healthcare, workplace, education) and identify where AI fits alongside human interpreting, captions, texting, and multilingual/multimodal practices – and where it increases or reduces friction. Second, a deployment dilemma group exercise: teams work through three common dilemmas regarding deployment selected from a menu, such as trust and over-reliance, authorship disclosure, consent and data use, access and pricing, human-in-the loop boundaries, and accountability/complaint procedures – and what these choices mean for broader deaf communication ecologies.
Presenters
Presenter 1
Name: Maartje De Meulder
Email: maartje.demeulder@hu.nl
Organization: University of Applied Sciences Utrecht
Bio:
Dr. Maartje De Meulder combines Deaf Studies, language policy, and the wider social sciences to make sense of how language technologies meet deaf lives – with a particular focus on sign language AI. She is interested in how technological affordances shape language, communication, and access, and how deaf people use these technologies in their everyday lives. Alongside her academic work, she regularly collaborates with deaf organisations, interpreting professionals, and technology stakeholders to help the think critically about key questions surrounding ethics, access, and governance of language technologies. Across her work, she aims to build bridges between research, policy and practice: developing concepts and evidence that make sense to deaf communities themselves, while also challenging dominant assumptions about “access” and “inclusion” in tech development, education, and public services.
Learning Objectives
By the end of the workshop, participants will be able to:
	1.	Use a Deaf Studies/social science lens to place sign language AI within Deaf communication ecologies (not just “translation”).
	2.	Assess power and inclusion in sign language AI: who benefits, who is left out.
	3.	Judge “good enough” trade-offs: when speed/scale is acceptable, and when quality must be non-negotiable.
	4.	Anticipate effects on interpreting and access: jobs, workflows, user choice, and friction.
	5.	Understand “sign languages as data”: consent, representation, and consequences.
	6.	Produce role-specific guardrails: transparency, deployment boundaries, pricing/access, and complaint/accountability processes.
ethics-where-does-it-stop:
Panel: Ethics: Where does it stop?      Host: Abraham Glasser
Panelist: Adam Munder, Maartje De Meulder, one or two more?
research-data-collection-partnerships:
Proposal Submission: Workshop
Duration: 50 minutes:  25 minutes presentation with (possible) 15 minutes group discussion, then 10 minutes for wrap up and  Q+As
Title and Description:  Research & Data Collection: Strengthening Validity Through Partnerships
The workshop will cover how data collection – of experiences by Deaf individuals – can help AI company credibility. Our university's study related to AI-generated ASL videos and data collection will be shared as an example, illustrating how university collaboration strengthens data quality, research rigor, and ethical accountability. The session will highlight examples of practices that center Deaf expertise in research. The workshop will provide an opportunity for participants to discuss ideas and practices.
Presenter(s): Pamela Macias
pamela.macias@colorado.edu
University of Colorado, Boulder; Assistant Professor in the ASL Program. CO-Principal Investigator of a study.

intentional-design-sl-translation-hybrid:
Title
Intentional Design for SL Translation: AI and Hybrid Approaches
Description
This workshop explores how AI can support Deaf access when designed and used thoughtfully. With a focus on real-time accessible communication, such as emergency and templated notifications, the session examines the role of automated sign language translation and when AI should be used alongside human-in-the-loop approaches. 
 
Participants will gain an understanding of why different contexts require different solutions and how combining AI with human expertise can support clearer, safer, and more Deaf-centered outcomes. The session will also highlight how human-in-the-loop approaches can operate in real time, allowing AI-generated messages to be reviewed, refined, and enhanced as communication needs evolve. The workshop encourages informed discussion about appropriate use and expectations of AI in sign language access.
Presenters
Presenter 1
Name: Noreen Wilson
Email: noreen@kara.tech
Organization: Kara Technologies
Bio:
Noreen Wilson is the Chief Delivery Officer at Kara Technologies. As a CODA, bilingual in American and New Zealand Sign Languages, she brings a unique cross-cultural lens to inclusive technology. With an extensive background in accessible resource leadership, sign language interpreting, and founding a translation company, she has comprehensive experience leading complex initiatives with strategic insight, collaboration, and a sharp eye for detail. Grounded in a Deaf-first mindset, Noreen focuses on AI-supported solutions that bridge accessibility gaps, working alongside the Deaf community to apply innovative, cutting-edge technology within meaningful and inclusive areas.
Presenter 2
Name: Molly Glass
Email: molly@kara.tech
Organization: Kara Technologies
Bio:
Molly Glass is a CDI Translation Specialist & Project Coordinator at Kara Technologies. She had both lived experience and specialized expertise in signed language access within emerging and innovative technologies. For the past three years, she has worked at Kara Technologies as an ASL Specialist, where she contributes to the development and quality assurance of sign language translations. Her goal is to provide Deaf centered solutions with a strong focus on usability, linguistic accuracy, and respect for Deaf community needs. 
Molly holds certificates in Deaf Interpreting and Signed Language Translation from the National Technical Institute for the Deaf (NTID). In addition to her professional work, she serves on the leadership team of a Deaf-run, international coalition advancing safe, ethical, fair, and accountable standards for the use of artificial intelligence in translation and interpreting. 
Presenter 3
Name: Yeh Kim
Email: yeh@kara.tech
Organization: Kara Technologies
Bio:
Yeh Kim is an AI Sign Linguistics and Community Engagement Advisor at Kara Technologies, bringing over eight years of experience in sign language interpreting and translation, seven years of teaching American Sign Language (ASL), and several years of teaching Korean Sign Language (KSL), along with graduate-level research experience in language and communication. 
Through close collaboration with Deaf professionals, educators, interpreters, and community organizations, Yeh helps ensure that sign language AI reflects authentic language use, cultural integrity, and real-world communication needs. His work centers Deaf and DeafDisabled communities by bridging linguistic expertise, accessibility practice, and Deaf-led AI development. At Kara, he supports the evaluation and refinement of automated sign language translation through linguistic review, database quality, and human-in-the-loop workflows, primarily in ASL.
Learning Objectives
By the end of this workshop, participants will be able to: 
1. Develop a shared understanding of automated, hybrid, and human-led approaches to signed language translation, including where each is appropriate and where caution is needed. 
2. Understand how human-in-the-loop models support and enhance quality, accountability, adaptability, and Deaf-centered outcomes within AI-supported areas. 
3. Engage in informed discussion about realistic expectations, limitations, and opportunities for AI in signed language access.
Technical Requirements
Good internet connection 
Spare power leads / HDMI connectors if needed
Projector/tv to present from
beyond-gloss-framework-sign-language-dat:
Beyond Gloss: A New Framework for Sign Language Data
Abstract: 
Beyond glosses: structured, machine-readable representations for sign languages.
Most Sign Language Translation and Sign Language Processing pipelines rely on glosses as an intermediate representation between visual input and spoken-language text. However, glosses constitute an inherently lossy representation and are poorly suited to encode core properties of sign languages, such as non-manual markers, spatial indexing, and information structure. Recent literature has explicitly highlighted the methodological and evaluative limitations of the gloss-based paradigm and called for linguistically grounded alternatives. At the same time, existing solutions include phonetic notations (e.g., HamNoSys) with machine-readable encodings for synthesis (e.g., SiGML), as well as writing systems such as SignWriting with computable encodings (FSW/SWU). These approaches, however, primarily address articulatory or graphical levels and do not provide an integrated, sentence-level representation suitable for neural learning and downstream reasoning. In this panel, we discuss the need for structured, declarative, and multi-level representations for sign languages that go beyond glosses. We introduce a proposal for a sparse, machine-readable schema designed to interface linguistic annotation, neural models, and applied systems, and we situate this approach within the broader landscape of computational linguistics, sign language research, and AI-driven accessibility technologies.
Presenter: Emanuele Chiusaroli
Names, emails:
Emanuele Chiusaroli, CEO and founder at Handy Signs, emanuele@handysigns.it,
Marta Sanzari, Head of Ai R&D at Handy Signs, marta@handysigns.it,
Organization: Handy Signs s.r.l., from Rome (Italy), startup founded in 2023
Brief presenter bio: Emanuele coordinates the development teams and handles funding. He is an experienced Project Manager and Scrum Master with 25 years of experience in Telco and 3 years in Enterprise Sales.
Learning objectives: The participant will learn about the company Handy Signs and the solutions developed so far, as well as its current and future vision. They will attend a live demonstration of the application, and participants who are familiar with Italian Sign Language will have the opportunity to try it out.

better-world-technology-shaped-by-deaf:
Presentation Title: A Better World - Driven by Technology, Shaped by the Deaf
Sławek Łuczywek and Ashod Derandonyan 
Migam is proud to present a vision for a "Better World - driven by technology, shaped by the Deaf." The core objective of this presentation is to illuminate the transformative opportunities unlocked by Artificial Intelligence (AI) in the complex domain of sign language translation and interpretation.The migam.ai Case Study
We will illustrate this potential through the lens of the migam.ai case study, which exemplifies cutting-edge AI technology applied to real-world communication challenges. This case will not just showcase technological capability but also highlight the critical need for a human-centric approach in its development and deployment.Integrating Deaf Expertise: A Holistic AI Production Process
The most crucial element of this presentation, and our philosophy, is the deep, essential integration of Deaf colleagues and experts throughout the entire AI production lifecycle. This is not merely an optional addition; it is a fundamental requirement for creating accurate, sensitive, and truly effective translation systems.
We will focus on various specific ways this integration is achieved, detailing the necessary collaboration points:
From Initial Design and Conceptualization: Ensuring that systems are built with an inherent understanding of the nuances, syntax, and cultural context of various sign languages, which can only be provided by native signers.
Data Curation and Annotation: Utilizing the expert linguistic knowledge of Deaf individuals to accurately label, verify, and enrich the vast datasets required to train robust AI models, thereby eliminating biases and inaccuracies.
System Testing and Validation: Engaging Deaf users as primary testers and evaluators to ensure the system’s output is fluent, contextually appropriate, and understandable to the sign language community.
Deployment and Communication: Establishing smooth, effective communication channels between the technology development world and the Deaf world to facilitate continuous feedback, adaptation, and successful adoption of the technology.
Ultimately, Migam’s approach is centered on the belief that AI serves as a powerful engine, but the lived experience, expertise, and guidance of the Deaf community are the indispensable hands that must shape and direct its output, ensuring that technology truly empowers and connects two worlds.
future-sl-translation-is-transcription:
Format: Workshop
Duration: 20 minutes
Title: The Future of Sign Language Translation is Transcription
Abstract:
Sign Language Processing has long been overlooked in mainstream language technology due to the challenges of bridging visual-gestural languages with text-based AI. In this talk, I will show how SignWriting—a universal transcription system—creates a structured, scalable bridge between video-based sign language input and spoken language text, redefining sign language translation and generation.
Leveraging this framework enables both accurate translation from sign language and natural generation into it, unlocking new possibilities for real-time, multilingual sign language applications. This approach makes AI-driven interactions more natural, accurate, and accessible.
I will demonstrate how SignWriting establishes a clear separation between Natural Language Processing and Computer Vision in Sign Language Processing. This division allows researchers to contribute within their expertise while fostering real-time, multilingual applications. By bridging text-based AI with the visual-gestural nature of signed languages, this approach paves the way for truly inclusive language technology.
Presenter(s): 
Name, email, organization: Dr. Amit Moryossef, amit@nagish.com, Nagish
Brief presenter bio: Dr. Amit Moryossef is a researcher and entrepreneur in sign-language technology. He completed his Ph.D. at Bar-Ilan University and a postdoc at the University of Zurich. He founded sign.mt, a real-time sign-language translation platform, which was recently acquired by Nagish, where he now leads research. His work has received multiple best paper awards at ACL and EMNLP, focusing on making signed languages accessible through machine learning.
Learning Objectives: Mindset shift, with the goal of thinking about sign language translation as a problem with the basic representation of sign language.
Technical Requirements: none
asl-ai-authority-deaf-experts:
ASL, AI, and Authority: Centering Deaf ASL Experts in Language Technologies
TWA Innovations: TWA Textbook, TWA Dictionary and TWA Academy
hello@truewayasl.com
Workshop Description:
As AI enthusiasm around sign language accelerates, American Sign Language is increasingly treated as a technical problem rather than a living language shaped by history, oppression, pedagogy, and community practice. This workshop argues that placing Deaf signers in front of cameras is not the same as placing Deaf ASL experts in decision-making and leadership roles - and that confusing the two has real consequences.
Led by Deaf ASL educators and digital language developers, this session examines the hidden labor behind ASL documentation: defining meaning without direct translation, navigating multimodal grammar, validating community usage, and resisting reductive labeling that flattens linguistic reality. The presenters draw on their daily work building Deaf-authored ASL textbooks and dictionaries to illustrate where AI projects most often fail - and why.
This workshop calls for a shift from representation to authority, urging AI researchers and engineers to collaborate with Deaf ASL experts, not merely extract data from Deaf bodies. Ethical ASL–AI work requires interdisciplinary leadership - not token inclusion.
Learning Objectives
By the end of this workshop, participants will be able to:
Distinguish between ASL fluency and ASL expertise, and explain why this distinction is critical in AI-driven language technologies.
Identify common failures in ASL–AI projects that result from excluding Deaf ASL educators and linguists from leadership and design decisions.
Describe the linguistic, pedagogical, and cultural complexities involved in documenting ASL beyond surface-level data collection.
Apply a Deaf-led, interdisciplinary framework for ethically developing ASL-related AI systems that center linguistic authority, community validation, and long-term impact.
Presenters:
Elisa Abenchuchan Vita
Elisa Abenchuchan Vita brings deep expertise in ASL linguistics, education, and language learning, with a strong commitment to protecting the integrity of sign languages as they intersect with technology. She holds a B.A. in English, an M.S. in Deaf Education, and an M.A. in Linguistics, and has extensive experience teaching English and ASL at the secondary level, as well as providing ASL instruction for hearing families with deaf children.
As the ASL Dictionary Curator and Project Manager for the TWA Dictionary, Elisa leads linguistic research on ASL vocabulary, documents community-used signs, leads filming processes, and organizes entries to ensure accuracy, consistency, and accessibility. Her work emphasizes the importance of community-driven documentation and careful curation—especially as ASL resources increasingly intersect with AI and digital systems. Elisa is passionate about advancing ASL documentation while safeguarding linguistic nuance in emerging technologies.
Lisa Gelineau
Lisa Gelineau brings over 30 years of experience in ASL education, specializing in college-level instruction, ASL proficiency evaluation, and curriculum development, with a long-standing focus on how language is taught, assessed, and represented in digital environments. She holds a Master’s degree in Deaf Studies/Deaf Education from Lamar University, completed all doctoral coursework in Deaf Studies at Lamar University, and earned a Bachelor’s degree in Communication Studies from Texas State University.
A co-founder and co-author of TWA Innovations since 2011, Lisa has worked collaboratively to develop one of the most comprehensive ASL curricula available, thoughtfully integrating research-based pedagogy with evolving educational technologies. She is also a full-time professor at Austin Community College (ACC), teaching ASL at all levels across face-to-face, hybrid, and online modalities. Her commitment to high-quality ASL education has been recognized with the Teacher Excellence Award from ACC for outstanding teaching. Lisa is particularly invested in ensuring that technological tools—including AI—support, rather than oversimplify, the complexity of signed languages.
Raychelle Harris
Raychelle Harris is a Deaf ASL educator, author, and co-owner of TWA Innovations, where she provides academic leadership for the TWA Textbook, TWA Dictionary, and TWA Academy. She holds a PhD in Education with an emphasis in linguistics, and her work focuses on ASL teaching methods, sign language assessment, and the ethical representation of signed languages in educational and digital systems.
For over a decade, Raychelle established, built, and led the world’s only Master’s program in Sign Language Education - at Gallaudet University, helping define global standards for sign language teacher preparation, curriculum design, and language assessment grounded in Deaf led knowledge and expertise.
In addition, Raychelle brings a strong background in research and research ethics, teaching graduate research ethics courses, serving on and chairing dissertation committees, advising researchers working with Deaf communities, and published on ethical research practices in Deaf-centered contexts. At TWA Innovations, she oversees academic integrity and the responsible integration of emerging technologies, including AI, advocating for Deaf-led expert authority and guarding against extractive, data-driven approaches that exclude Deaf experts from leadership and decision-making.
Shelley Oishi
Shelley Oishi is the CEO of TWA Innovations and a leader in advancing accessible, language-centered technology for the Deaf community. With a background spanning rehabilitation counseling, business development, and accessibility-focused program leadership, Shelley has been instrumental in bringing TWA Innovations to the forefront of digital innovation—including the TWA Textbook, the TWA Dictionary app, and the forthcoming TWA Academy.
Shelley holds a B.A. in Graphic Design from the Rochester Institute of Technology and an M.S. in Rehabilitation Counseling from Western Oregon University. Her professional experience includes senior leadership roles at Communication Service for the Deaf and Purple Communications, where she led large teams, built strategic partnerships, and supported accessibility initiatives across education, government, and nonprofit sectors
At TWA Innovations, Shelley focuses on the responsible integration of technology—particularly AI—into sign language resources, advocating for systems that respect linguistic accuracy, community knowledge, and ethical design.
Technical Requirements:
A high-brightness projector or large display with HDMI connectivity, capable of clearly displaying video-intensive slides in bright or high-ambient-light conditions.
sign-language-ai-international-policy:
Sign Language AI and International Policy Spaces 
Joseph J. Murray, President, World Federation of the Deaf 
This 30-minute session will present the World Federation of the Deaf’s (WFD) emerging work at the intersection of artificial intelligence, sign languages, and international policy development. Presenting from the  perspective of a global representative organization, the session will outline key trends in AI governance affecting sign language communities. The presentation will also highlight how international policy spaces — including multilateral forums, standards bodies, and global disability rights discussions — are shaping the future of AI and sign languages. 
The session will report on findings from recent WFD initiatives, including an in-person survey of WFD Ordinary Members documenting national-level experiences, concerns, and policy priorities related to AI and sign language technologies. Through concrete examples drawn from multiple regions, the presentation will identify common challenges, promising practices, and gaps requiring coordinated international advocacy. 
The goal is to equip participants with a clearer understanding of global deaf perspectives on AI while fostering dialogue on how researchers, developers, policymakers, and deaf organizations can collaborate to ensure that AI development respects linguistic rights, human rights frameworks, and deaf community leadership on AI. 
Presenter biography: Dr. Joseph J. Murray is President of the World Federation of the Deaf and Board Member of the International Disability Alliance. Dr. Murray has 30+ years of international disability rights experience and has worked as an advisor for NGOs and deaf and disability communities in over 50 different countries, including, most recently, as member of the official Norwegian government commission for sign language.  Dr. Murray is Professor of Deaf Studies at Gallaudet University and has published widely in human rights, linguistic rights, deaf studies and applied linguistics.
fireside-chat-fcc: Travis Dougherty will be discussing with Suzy Rosen Singletonfrom FCC on a variety of topics from AI regulation from government POV, emergency wireless act, and upcoming rulings that the SLxAI industry will benefit from.    This will be only a 20 minute chat and 10 min of QA sessions.  30 minutes total at max.  If this workshop is not selected, Suzy Rosen Singletonwill not be able to attend.  It is FCC’s policy to allow their staff to go if they have a part in the workshops in any conferences.Travis Dougherty was Maryland Relay for Telecommunications Access of Maryland for 5 years before becoming a full-time CEO with GoSign.AI     He was involved with the National Association of State Relay Administration and elected as Member at Large, then Vice Chair, and finally as the Chair.  He worked with different state governments and federal agencies.  He served on Deaf and Hard of Hearing Consumer Advocacy Network (DHHCAN) and Safe AI Taskforce as NASRA representative.  Also, he served on FCC’s Disability Advisory Committee 5th term.  He also coauthored “Virginia Relay Digital Modernization” which was published in January 2026  https://rga.lis.virginia.gov/Published/2026/RD130/PDFSuzy Rosen Singleton, a native user of American Sign Language, is the Chief of the Disability Rights Office of the Consumer and Governmental Affairs Bureau of the Federal Communications Commission, and has practiced with the Commission since 2012. Her legal career started in 1992, and involved civil rights litigation for the now defunct California Center for Law and the Deaf (CalCLAD), and disability policy work in the following former capacities: as the counsel for government affairs for the National Association of the Deaf (NAD), a special education law compliance officer for the U.S. Department of Education, and the ombuds for Gallaudet University. Suzy is licensed to practice in the District of Columbia, and holds a Juris Doctor from the UCLA School of Law, and a Bachelor of Science in Political Science from the University of California, Berkeley.
coset-safe-ai-communication-success:
Title:  
CoSET SAFE AI: Designing for Communication Success 
Presenters (5 total): 
Name: Abraham Glasser 
Email: abraham.glasser@gallaudet.edu 
Organization: Coalition for Sign Language Equity in Technology (CoSET) 
Bio: Dr. Abraham Glasser is an Assistant Professor in the MS/PhD Accessible Human 
Centered Computing and Policy (AHCP) program at Gallaudet University. He is an active 
member of CoSET. 
Name: Tim Riker 
Email: tim.riker@coset.org 
Organization: CoSET 
Bio: Tim Riker is a Certified Deaf Interpreter, educator, and community advocate serving on 
the Coalition on Sign Language Equity in Technology (CoSET). He works at the intersection 
of Deaf-led design, language justice, and AI, advancing ethical, accessible technologies 
that center Deaf, DeafBlind, and multilingual communities through collaboration, 
research, and systems change. 
Name: Stephanie Kent 
Email: steph.kent@coset.org 
Organization: CoSET 
Bio: Steph is an action researcher of communication, interpreting and social change; and 
founder of the Learning Lab for Resiliency®. 
Name: Celena Ponce 
Email: celena.ponce@coset.org 
Organization: CoSET 
Bio: Celena Ponce holds a BS in Electrical Engineering with a focus in Digital Sign 
Processing and Masters in Electrical Engineering and Computer Science with a focus on 
sign language recognition. She runs a disability- and immigrant-forward nonprofit called 
Hands United. 
Name: Jeff Shaul 
Email: jeff.shaul@coset.org 
Organization: CoSET 
Bio: Jeff Shaul is a Deaf backend engineer building sign language–first systems. At Sign-
Speak, he develops production platforms for language technologies. His background 
spans large-scale data systems and software engineering. He is a part owner of GoSign.AI 
and contributes to CoSET’s work on ethical evaluation frameworks for sign language AI. 
Proposal description: 
This 50-minute workshop will introduce the Coalition for Sign Language Equity in 
Technology (CoSET), giving an overview of who we are, what we’ve accomplished, and 
what we aim to do with sign languages and artificial intelligence (AI). We’ll describe the 3D 
Risk Framework introduced in the AI Interpreting Solutions Evaluation Toolkit Part A: 
Organization, Implementation, and Management and the Hazard Triangle and SAFE Zone 
which is illustrated in the Toolkit Part B: Technical Specifications. The presentation will 
include our Vision, Mission, and Purpose to institute an objective technical harness and 
innovative social testbed for the identification of risk factors that must be managed in 
communication technologies intended to provide language access in live, real-world 
human-to-human interactions. We will provide ample time for Q&A.
